searchDocs.title: Scope​
searchDocs.content: </> interface Scope { Bind: ( self, state: State<T>, callback: (newValue: T?) → () ) → ()-- Binds to a State scoped to this component's lifecycle BindProperty: ( self, state: State<T>, instance: Instance , property: string ) → ()-- Binds a State directly to a named property on an instance BindEvent: ( self, instance: Instance , eventName: string, callback: (...any) → () ) → ()-- Connects an instance event scoped to this component's lifecycle Mount: ( self, component: Component, instance: Instance , properties: {[string]: any}? ) → ()-- Mounts a child component scoped to this component's lifecycle MountWhen: ( self, condition: State<boolean>, component: Component, instance: Instance , properties: {[string]: any}? ) → ()-- Conditionally mounts a child component based on state } A minimal lifecycle scope passed to each component function. Use Bind to subscribe to reactive state, BindProperty to drive a named instance property directly from state, BindEvent to connect instance events, Mount to compose child components, and MountWhen for conditional rendering. All bindings, event connections, and child mounts made through this scope are automatically cleaned up when the parent GUI is unmounted.

searchDocs.title: Component​
searchDocs.content: </> type Component = ( instance: Instance , scope: Scope, properties: {[string]: any}? ) → () A reusable function that applies behavior to a GUI child Instance. Components are mounted to a specific Instance in the GUI hierarchy and can bind to reactive state using the provided Scope. Components should be used for reusable pieces of UI behavior. For example, you might have a "HealthBar" component that you mount to any Frame to turn it into a health bar, or a "Draggable" component that makes any GUI element draggable. Components should not manage the lifecycle of their own Instance (i.e. by destroying it or reparenting it), they should only be responsible for updating the instance's properties in response to state changes, passed down through properties or observed via Bind. Let the parent Controller manage the lifecycle of all instances.

searchDocs.title: State​
searchDocs.content: </> interface State {} A reactive state object. Create these using Flux:State and pass them to Controller:Bind or Scope:Bind inside components to reactively drive UI updates.

searchDocs.title: AnimatedState​
searchDocs.content: </> interface AnimatedState {} A read-only animated state returned by Flux:Tween and Flux:Spring. Supports Get, Observe, and Destroy — compatible with Bind and BindProperty. Call Destroy when you are finished with it to stop the animation and free resources.

searchDocs.title: Self​
searchDocs.content: </> type Self = Flux

searchDocs.title: State​
searchDocs.content: </> Flux.State( _self: Flux, initialValue: T?-- An optional starting value ) → State<T> Creates a new reactive State object that can be bound to GUI updates. Call Set on the returned object to update the state and trigger UI updates. local coins: Flux.State<number> = Flux:State(0) controller:Bind(coins, function(n: number) label.Text = tostring(n) end) coins:Set(42)

searchDocs.title: Computed​
searchDocs.content: </> Flux.Computed( _self: Flux, callback: () → T,-- A function that derives the computed value from current dependencies dependencies: {State<any>}?-- Optional array of states to observe; if omitted, the callback is evaluated once ) → State<T> Creates a reactive derived state value. The callback runs immediately to seed the value, then re-runs whenever any dependency changes. The returned state is read-only from the caller perspective and is cleaned up automatically when its dependencies are destroyed.

searchDocs.title: Compose​
searchDocs.content: </> Flux.Compose( _self: Flux, components: {Component}-- Components to run in sequence for the same instance and scope ) → Component Composes multiple components into one component. The returned component invokes each component in order using the same instance, scope, and properties. local FancyButton = Flux:Compose({ HoverEffect, ClickEffect, PulseEffect, }) controller:Mount(FancyButton, button, {theme = themeState})

searchDocs.title: Tween​
searchDocs.content: </> Flux.Tween( _self: Flux, source: State<T>,-- The reactive state to animate tweenInfo: TweenInfo -- Controls duration, easing style, and easing direction ) → AnimatedState<T> Returns an AnimatedState that smoothly interpolates toward source whenever it changes, using TweenInfo for timing and easing. Supports number and any Roblox type with a :Lerp method (UDim2, Vector2, Vector3, Color3, CFrame, UDim). Call Destroy when finished. local health = Flux:State(100) local animHealth = Flux:Tween(health, TweenInfo.new(0.4, Enum.EasingStyle.Quad)) scope:BindProperty(animHealth, bar, "Size") health:Set(50)

searchDocs.title: Spring​
searchDocs.content: </> Flux.Spring( _self: Flux, source: State<T>,-- The reactive state to animate speed: number,-- Angular frequency — how quickly the spring responds (e.g. 15–25 for snappy UI) damping: number-- Damping ratio — 1 is critically damped (no overshoot), < 1 is bouncy ) → AnimatedState<T> Returns an AnimatedState driven by spring physics that follows source whenever it changes. Supports number, Vector2, Vector3, Color3, and UDim2. The Heartbeat connection is only active while the spring is moving and disconnects automatically when settled. Call Destroy when finished. local open = Flux:State(UDim2.fromScale(0, 0)) local animOpen = Flux:Spring(open, 20, 1) scope:BindProperty(animOpen, panel, "Size") open:Set(UDim2.fromScale(1, 1))

searchDocs.title: MountScreenGui​
searchDocs.content: </> Flux.MountScreenGui( _self: Flux, gui: ScreenGui -- The GUI instance to mount ) → Controller Parents the given ScreenGui to the local player's PlayerGui and returns a Controller that owns its lifecycle. The caller is responsible for cloning templates before passing them in.

searchDocs.title: MountSurfaceGui​
searchDocs.content: </> Flux.MountSurfaceGui( _self: Flux, gui: SurfaceGui ,-- The GUI instance to mount adornee: BasePart ,-- The part the SurfaceGui will be attached to face: Enum.NormalId?-- The face of the part the SurfaceGui will be attached to (optional) ) → Controller Sets the Adornee (and optionally Face) on the given SurfaceGui, parents it to the local player's PlayerGui, and returns a Controller that owns its lifecycle. The caller is responsible for cloning templates before passing them in.

searchDocs.title: MountBillboardGui​
searchDocs.content: </> Flux.MountBillboardGui( _self: Flux, gui: BillboardGui ,-- The GUI instance to mount adornee: BasePart -- The part the BillboardGui will be attached to ) → Controller Sets the Adornee on the given BillboardGui, parents it to the local player's PlayerGui, and returns a Controller that owns its lifecycle. The caller is responsible for cloning templates before passing them in.

searchDocs.title: Scope​
searchDocs.content: </> interface Scope { Bind: ( self, state: State<T>, callback: (newValue: T?) → () ) → ()-- Binds to a State scoped to this component's lifecycle BindProperty: ( self, state: State<T>, instance: Instance , property: string ) → ()-- Binds a State directly to a named property on an instance BindEvent: ( self, instance: Instance , eventName: string, callback: (...any) → () ) → ()-- Connects an instance event scoped to this component's lifecycle Mount: ( self, component: Component, instance: Instance , properties: {[string]: any}? ) → ()-- Mounts a child component scoped to this component's lifecycle MountWhen: ( self, condition: State<boolean>, component: Component, instance: Instance , properties: {[string]: any}? ) → ()-- Conditionally mounts a child component based on state } A lifecycle scope passed to each component function. Use Bind to subscribe to reactive state, BindProperty to drive a named instance property directly from state, BindEvent to connect instance events, Mount to compose child components, and MountWhen for conditional rendering. All bindings, event connections, and child mounts made through this scope are automatically cleaned up when the parent GUI is unmounted.

searchDocs.title: Component​
searchDocs.content: </> type Component = ( instance: Instance , scope: Scope, properties: {[string]: any}? ) → () A reusable function that applies behavior to a GUI child Instance. Components are mounted to a specific Instance in the GUI hierarchy and can bind to reactive state using the provided Scope. Components should be used for reusable pieces of UI behavior. For example, you might have a "HealthBar" component that you mount to any Frame to turn it into a health bar, or a "Draggable" component that makes a GUI element draggable. Components should not manage the lifecycle of their own Instance (i.e. by destroying it or reparenting it), they should only be responsible for updating the instance's properties in response to state changes, passed down through properties or observed via Bind. Let the parent Controller manage the lifecycle of all instances.

searchDocs.title: State​
searchDocs.content: </> interface State {} A reactive state object. Create these using Flux:State and pass them to Controller:Bind or Scope:Bind inside components to reactively drive UI updates.

searchDocs.title: Self​
searchDocs.content: </> type Self = Controller

searchDocs.title: new​
searchDocs.content: </> Controller.new( gui: ScreenGui | SurfaceGui | BillboardGui -- The GUI instance to manage ) → Controller-- A new Controller for the given GUI instance Creates a new Controller for the given GUI instance.

searchDocs.title: Unmount​
searchDocs.content: </> Controller.Unmount(self: Self) → () Destroys the GUI Instance and disconnects all bindings. This should be called when you are finished with the GUI to properly clean up resources and prevent memory leaks. local controller = Flux:MountScreenGui(gui) controller:Unmount() -- Clean up when done

searchDocs.title: Destroy​
searchDocs.content: </> Controller.Destroy(self: Self) → () Compatibility alias for Unmount. Calling Destroy and Unmount has identical behavior.

searchDocs.title: GetInstance​
searchDocs.content: </> Controller.GetInstance(self: Self) → ScreenGui | SurfaceGui | BillboardGui -- The managed GUI instance Returns the GUI instance managed by this controller. local gui = controller:GetInstance() local frame = gui:FindFirstChild("Frame")

searchDocs.title: Bind​
searchDocs.content: </> Controller.Bind( self: Self, state: State<T>,-- The reactive state to observe callback: (newValue: T?) → ()-- Function to call with the current and future values ) → () Binds to a State object so that callback fires immediately with the current value, and then again every time the state changes. The binding is automatically cleaned up when the controller is unmounted. local coins: Flux.State<number> = Flux:State(0) controller:Bind(coins, function(count: number) local label = controller:GetInstance():FindFirstChild("CoinsLabel") if label and label:IsA("TextLabel") then label.Text = tostring(count) end end)

searchDocs.title: BindProperty​
searchDocs.content: </> Controller.BindProperty( self: Self, state: State<T>,-- The reactive state to observe instance: Instance ,-- The GUI instance to update property: string-- The name of the property to bind to ) → () Binds a State directly to a named property on an instance. The property is updated immediately with the current state value and then again every time the state changes. local size = Flux:State(UDim2.fromScale(0.5, 0.5)) controller:BindProperty(size, frame, "Size") size:Set(UDim2.fromScale(1, 1)) -- frame.Size updates automatically

searchDocs.title: BindEvent​
searchDocs.content: </> Controller.BindEvent( self: Self, instance: Instance ,-- The GUI instance whose event to connect to eventName: string,-- The name of the event (e.g. "Activated", "MouseEnter") callback: (...any) → ()-- Function to call when the event fires ) → () Connects to a named event on an instance with automatic cleanup. The connection is automatically disconnected when the controller is unmounted. local button = controller:GetInstance():FindFirstChild("Button") if button then controller:BindEvent(button, "Activated", function() print("Button clicked!") end) end

searchDocs.title: Mount​
searchDocs.content: </> Controller.Mount( self: Self, component: Component,-- The component function to apply instance: Instance ,-- The GUI instance to pass to the component properties: {[string]: any}?-- Optional properties table to pass to the component ) → () Applies a reusable Component function to a child instance. The component receives a Scope that allows it to bind to reactive state. All bindings made by the component are automatically cleaned up when the controller is unmounted. local function HealthBar(instance: Instance, scope: Flux.Scope, properties: {health: Flux.State<number>}) scope:Bind(properties.health, function(hp: number) local bar: Frame = instance :: Frame bar.Size = UDim2.fromScale(hp / 100, 1) end) end local healthState = Flux:State(100) local healthBarFrame = controller:GetInstance():FindFirstChild("HealthBarFrame") if healthBarFrame then controller:Mount(HealthBar, healthBarFrame, {health = healthState}) end

searchDocs.title: MountWhen​
searchDocs.content: </> Controller.MountWhen( self: Self, condition: State<boolean>,-- A boolean state that drives visibility component: Component,-- The component function to conditionally mount instance: Instance ,-- The GUI instance to pass to the component properties: {[string]: any}?-- Optional properties table to pass to the component ) → () Conditionally mounts a component based on a boolean state. When the condition becomes true, the component is mounted and receives its own scope. When it becomes false, the component and all its bindings are cleaned up. If the condition becomes true again, the component is mounted again with a fresh scope (it does not resume prior local state). The target instance remains in the hierarchy; only component behavior is mounted/unmounted. Only one mounted component scope is active at a time. local isVisible = Flux:State(false) local function Panel(instance: Instance, scope: Flux.Scope) local text = Flux:State("Hello") scope:BindProperty(text, instance, "Text") end local panelFrame = controller:GetInstance():FindFirstChild("Panel") if panelFrame then controller:MountWhen(isVisible, Panel, panelFrame) end isVisible:Set(true) -- Component mounts isVisible:Set(false) -- Component unmounts
